title: Repairing a larger plasterboard hole
subtitle: Nogging, patch, fill, skim
excerpt: How to repair a hole in a plasterboard wall that is too large for a self-adhesive patch: fitting a nogging between the studs to give a fixing edge, cutting and screwing in a new piece of plasterboard, taping the joints with scrim, filling and skimming flush.
sourceNotes: Standard UK drywall repair practice from the British Gypsum White Book and the City & Guilds Plastering Level 2 syllabus. Nogging fixing technique from the Timber Research and Development Association guidance on stud-wall repairs.

content.text: A hole larger than about 100 mm in a
plasterboard
wall cannot be bridged by a self-adhesive patch; it needs a piece of new board cut to fit and screwed to a solid backing. The key step is fitting a
nogging
behind the opening so the patch board has an edge to fix to. Once the patch is fixed, the repair follows the same scrim-tape-and-fill sequence as any plasterboard joint.
What you'll need
Piece of 50 × 50 mm timber for nogging.
Cut to fit between the studs, or long enough to span the opening.
Piece of 12.5 mm plasterboard.
Cut slightly smaller than the opening so it drops in and screws to the nogging edges.
Plasterboard screws, 38 mm.
For fixing the patch board to the noggins.
Scrim tape, jointing compound, taping knife.
For the fill and finishing sequence.
Finishing plaster and trowel.
For the skim coat.
Preparing the opening
Step 1, square off the hole.

Cut the ragged hole back to a clean rectangle using the utility knife and a steel rule. Cut to the nearest studs on each side if the hole is wide enough to reach them; otherwise cut a rectangle that stops clear of any studs. The sides of the rectangle should be clean and the plasterboard paper unfrayed.
Step 2, fit the noggings.

Cut two pieces of 50 × 50 mm timber, each 100 mm longer than the height of the opening. Slide one piece in behind each vertical edge of the opening, positioned so half its face is behind the existing plasterboard and half is exposed to take the patch. Drive screws through the existing plasterboard at an angle into the nogging to clamp it in place. A helper holding the nogging from behind is useful; if working alone, a long stick or piece of scrap timber holds it while you screw.
Fitting the patch
Step 3, cut the patch board.

Cut the patch board to exactly fit the opening (measure precisely; the board should sit flush with the face of the existing board). Place the patch in the opening paper-face outward and drive plasterboard screws at 150 mm centres along all four edges into the noggings. The screw heads should sit just below the paper face without tearing it.
Taping and filling
Step 4, apply scrim tape.
Run scrim tape along all four joints between the patch board and the existing wall. Press it firmly onto the surface.
Step 5, apply two fill coats.

Follow the same scrim-bedding and fill sequence as for any plasterboard joint: first coat embeds the tape and levels to the board face; second coat builds the fill and extends the feathered edge. Allow each coat to dry fully. Sand between coats with the sanding block or pole.
Step 6, apply the skim coat.

Prime the patch area and the fill coats with diluted PVA; allow to go tacky. Apply a 2 mm skim coat of finishing plaster over the patch and into the surrounding plaster. Tighten and polish in two passes as described for any skim coat. Allow to dry fully, then paint.
Common mistakes
Patch board rattling or flexing when pushed.

The noggings were not tight against the existing board. Remove the patch and tighten the nogging screws. Alternatively, apply a bead of wood adhesive between the nogging face and the patch board before re-screwing.
Crack re-appearing at the joint after six months.

The patch is moving slightly. This usually means the noggings are not fully rigid, or the original hole cuts through a stud and the patch was not screwed into the stud. Reopen the repair, add an additional nogging, and re-tape.
